title: Single inheritance and super()

overview.what: Inheritance allows a new class to inherit attributes and methods from an existing class, establishing an 'is-a' relationship. The `super()` function returns a proxy object that delegates method calls to a parent or sibling class. Think of inheritance like a master template and `super()` as a phone line explicitly calling the master template to avoid repeating setup logic. When overriding `__init__`, calling `super().__init__()` ensures the parent's initialization runs.
overview.why: It reduces code duplication by centralizing shared logic in base classes, making it easier to maintain and extend functionality.

why_learning_this: Understanding how third-party framework classes (like Django Views) work under the hood
Avoiding duplicated boilerplate across related data structures

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to call super() in __init__
common_mistakes.explanation: If a child class overrides `__init__` but forgets `super().__init__()`, the parent's attributes are never initialized, leading to `AttributeError` when those attributes are accessed.

common_mistakes.title: Redefining parent behavior instead of extending it
common_mistakes.explanation: Completely overwriting a parent method without calling `super()` drops the parent's original functionality when you only meant to add to it.

recall_questions.q: What is the primary purpose of the `super()` function in Python?
recall_questions.answer: It delegates method calls to a parent class, allowing a subclass to reuse the parent's logic instead of repeating it.

recall_questions.q: What happens if you override the `__init__` method in a child class but do not call `super().__init__()`?
recall_questions.answer: The parent class's initialization logic is skipped, which can cause an `AttributeError` if inherited methods try to access attributes that were supposed to be set up by the parent.

recall_questions.q: What kind of relationship does inheritance establish between a parent and child class?

practice_tasks.title: Extend an API Request class
practice_tasks.prompt: Modify the `SecureRequest` class so it takes a `url` and a `token`. Call `super().__init__(url)` to handle the URL, then store the `token` as an instance variable.

aha_moment.prediction: What does this print: True or False?
aha_moment.why: Because `NotFoundError` overrides `__init__` and does NOT call `super().__init__(code)`, the `APIError`'s `__init__` is skipped, so `self.code` is never set.

challenge.title: Implement a Database Model hierarchy
challenge.prompt: Create a base class `Model` with a `save` method printing 'saving to db'. Create a `UserModel` inheriting from `Model` whose `save` prints 'validating user' then calls the parent's `save` using `super()`.

understanding_checks.question: What happens when a child class overrides `__init__` but forgets to call `super().__init__()`?
understanding_checks.answer: The parent's initialization logic will be skipped.
understanding_checks.why: This is dangerous because any attributes that the parent normally sets up in its `__init__` won't be created, leading to an `AttributeError` when other inherited methods try to access them.

understanding_checks.question: What does this code print?
understanding_checks.why: The call to `b.ping()` runs the `ping` method in `B`. Inside `B.ping`, `super().ping()` delegates to the parent class `A`, printing 'A'. Then it resumes in `B` and prints 'B'.
